> e. Proof of Theorem E.2

Finally, we are ready to prove Theorem E.2. 

Proof of Theorem E.2. Let L be any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity S, sample complexity M , and input form I = [ N ] × { 0, 1}b × { 0, 1} such that given data generated from DNg,T (o), L outputs 

f (o) with probability at least 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8. Since f : {0, 1}N → { 0, 1}, its query complexity satisfies QδC ≤ N .If M > 2N T b , then we immediately have 

M S ≥ M > 2N T b ≥ 2QδC T b = Ω( QδC T b ) (E74) as required. Suppose otherwise that M ≤ 2N T b . From Theorem E.25, we know that there exists a randomized parallel decision tree A with query complexity Q = O(M S/ (T b )) such that given any input o ∈ { 0, 1}N , A outputs a random bitstring π whose distribution is 2 −ηb/ 8-close in total variation distance to the distribution of the transcript of L when given data generated from DNg,T (o). In particular, let a be the last bit of the transcript of 

L, which is the output of L. Then the last bit of π must be equal to a with probability at least 1 − 2−ηb/ 8. On the other hand, Pr[ a = f (o)] ≥ 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8 from the guarantee of L. Thus we have that the last bit of π is 92 equal to f (o) with probability at least 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8 − 2ηb/ 8 = 1 − δ by the union bound. This gives us a query algorithm (executing A and outputting the last bit of its output) with query complexity Q = O(M S/ (T b )) that outputs f (o) with probability at least 1 − δ. From the definition of δ-error classical randomized query complexity, we have 

QδC ≤ Q = O

 M S T b 



. (E75) Therefore, we arrive at M S ≥ Ω( QδC T b ), as required. This completes the proof of Theorem E.2. 

4. Bootstrap with one more time scale 

In this section, we build upon Theorem E.2 and bootstrap it to a much stronger lower bound by introducing a new task called dynamic Noisy Oracle Property Estimation (dynamic NOPE). We design this new task by adding one more time scale into the original NOPE. We call it dynamic NOPE, because the oracle becomes dynamic: it changes over time, but the property that we want to estimate stays fixed. Recall that our interested oracle property is given by a property function f : O → { 0, 1}, O ⊆ { 0, 1}N .To make the oracle dynamic, we consider randomly sampled oracles from two distributions p0, p 1 supported on 

O ⊆ { 0, 1}N , where o ∼ pB satisfies f (o) = B for B ∈ { 0, 1}. The relevant notion of query complexity in this case is the (1 /3)-error classical distributional query complexity, which we denote as QC . We introduce noise in the same way as before. We consider a noisy encoding function g : {0, 1}b ×{ 0, 1}b → { 0, 1} with encoding length 

b = ⌈c log N ⌉ and discrepancy disc( g) ≤ 2−ηb , where c = (865 /η 2) log ␀865 /η 2 for some constant η ∈ (0 .1, 2]. We will call such encoding functions good noisy encoding functions .In a dynamic setting, we introduce two time scales T1, T 2 and another parameter L that characterize how dynamic the oracle can be. They are positive integers and their relation with N will be specified later. We consider a 2-level hierarchical data generation process DNg,f (B) that depends on the binary property B ∈ { 0, 1}

we want to estimate:

The situation β ∈ [L] labels which oracle we are currently getting samples from, and α ∈ { 0, 1} specifies which part of the noisy encoding we are looking at. The data sample is of the form

Task E.26 (Dynamic Noisy Oracle Property Estimation (dynamic NOPE)) . Let N, T 1 be positive in-tegers. Let f : O → { 0, 1} be a function that specifies the target property for a set of possible oracles 

O ⊆ { 0, 1}N . Let g : {0, 1}b × { 0, 1}b → { 0, 1} be a noisy encoding function. The task of dynamic Noisy Oracle Property Estimation is to calculate the desired property B ∈ { 0, 1} using data samples generated from the noisy hierarchical data generation process DN,T 1 

> g,f

(B), for any B ∈ { 0, 1}.93 Intuitively, in dynamic NOPE, the target property B that we are trying to estimate is the parity of the length-L bitstring γ. In order to predict the parity of γ, we effectively need to predict all its L components 

γj , j ∈ [L] simultaneously. Meanwhile, information about γj is encoded as the property of the generated oracle 

oj . Hence, we are trying to solve L independent instances of NOPE described in Theorem E.2. Dynamic NOPE is hard to solve, because Theorem E.2 tells us that if the classical learning algorithm does not have enough memory size (determined by N, Q C ), its success probability on a single NOPE instance is at most 2 /3 + o(1) ≤ 1/2 + δ for some δ. We will build upon this and show that its success probability of solving dynamic NOPE is at most 1 /2 + (2 δ)L = 1 /2 + N −Ω(log( N )) . Finally, we use a hybrid argument to convert this success probability upper bound into a sample complexity lower bound of N Ω(log N ) = N ω(1) . Together, this means that any classical learning algorithm with insufficient memory would need a tremendous amount of samples to solve dynamic NOPE. Formally, we prove the following two classical hardness results. They hold for any T1 of our choice. The first result shows that given only the data from one refreshing block (i.e., M = τD where τD = T1T2 is the refreshing time of DN,T 1 

> g,f

(B)), any classical learning algorithm with insufficient size cannot perform much better than random guessing at dynamic NOPE. 

Theorem E.27 (Classical single-block hardness of dynamic NOPE) . Let N, T 1 be large integers. Let 

f : O → { 0, 1}, O ⊆ { 0, 1}N be a function that specifies the target property with (1 /3) -error classical distributional query complexity QC ≥ Ω( T 21 log 2(N ) log log( N )) . Let g be a good noisy encoding function. Then, for any randomized classical learning algorithm L with sample complexity M = τD , if its space complexity

Theorem E.28 (Classical sample complexity of dynamic NOPE) . Let N, T 1 be large integers. Let 

f : O → { 0, 1}, O ⊆ { 0, 1}N be a function that specifies the target property with (1 /3) -error classical distributional query complexity QC ≥ Ω( T 21 log 2(N ) log log( N )) . Let g be a good noisy encoding function. Then, for any randomized classical learning algorithm L that solves dynamic NOPE with probability at least 2/3, if its space complexity

The remaining parts of this section are devoted to prove Theorems E.27 and E.28. Since we are randomly sampling oracles, we first prove a distributional version of the sample-space lower bound in Section E 4 a. Then, in Sections E 4 b to E 4 e, we use a derandomization technique to prove a learning XOR lemma that suppresses the advantage of any algorithm exponentially. In Section E 4 f, we use a hybrid argument to show that an super-polynomially small advantage leads to a super-polynomial sample complexity. Finally, we complete the proof in Section E 4 g.  

> a. Distributional sample-space lower bound

We begin by noting that the sample-space lower bound we prove for NOPE (Theorem E.2) applies only to algorithms that are required to work for any input oracle o ∈ { 0, 1}N . But in dynamic NOPE, the learning 94 algorithm only needs to succeed on average for random oracles drawn from p0, p 1. Nevertheless, we can still prove a sample-space lower bound as follows by replacing randomized query complexity with distributional query complexity. 

Lemma E.29 (Distributional sample-space lower bound) . Let N be a large integer. Let the time scale T

be a positive integer. Let η ∈ (0 , 2] be a constant and c = (865 /η 2) log ␀865 /η 2. Let b ≥ c log N . Let g :

{0, 1}b × { 0, 1}b → { 0, 1} be an encoding map with disc( g) ≤ 2−ηb . Let f : O → { 0, 1}, O ⊆ { 0, 1}N be any function with δ-error classical distributional query complexity QδC with respect to the distributions p0, p 1 supported on O. Let L be any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity S, sample complexity M , and input form I = [ N ] × { 0, 1}b × { 0, 1} such that for any γ ∈ { 0, 1}, given data generated from DNg,T (o), o ∼ pγ , L

outputs γ with probability at least 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8. Then, L must satisfy 

M S ≥ Ω( QδC T b ). (E83) 

Proof of Theorem E.29. The proof resembles the proof of Theorem E.2. Let L be any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity S, sample complexity M , and input form I = [ N ] × { 0, 1}b × { 0, 1}

such that for any γ ∈ { 0, 1}, given data generated from DNg,T (o), o ∼ pγ , L outputs γ with probability at least 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8. Since f : {0, 1}N → { 0, 1}, its distributional query complexity satisfies QδC ≤ N . If M > 2N T b ,then we immediately have M S ≥ M > 2N T b ≥ 2QδC T b = Ω( QδC T b ) as required. Suppose otherwise that M ≤ 2N T b . From Theorem E.25, we know that there exists a randomized parallel decision tree A with query complexity Q = O(M S/ (T b )) such that given any input o ∈ { 0, 1}N , A outputs a random bitstring π whose distribution is 2 −ηb/ 8-close in total variation distance to the distribution of the transcript of L when given data generated from DNg,T (o). In particular, let a be the last bit of the transcript of 

L, which is the output of L. Then the last bit of π must be equal to a with probability at least 1 − 2−ηb/ 8. On the other hand, Pr[ a = γ] ≥ 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8. Thus we have that the last bit of π is equal to γ with probability at least 1 − δ + 2 −ηb/ 8 − 2ηb/ 8 = 1 − δ. This gives us a query algorithm (executing A and outputting the last bit of its output) with query complexity Q = O(M S/ (T b )) that outputs γ with probability at least 1 − δ, when the input oracle is drawn randomly from pγ . From the definition of δ-error classical distributional query complexity, we have 

QδC ≤ Q = O

 M S T b 



. (E84) Therefore, we arrive at M S ≥ Ω( QδC T b ), as required. In the following, we focus on the case of T = N as in dynamic NOPE.  

> b. Learning XOR Lemma

Theorem E.29 shows that if our classical machine does not have enough size, it cannot solve a single problem instance of NOPE. In dynamic NOPE, we further reduce the advantage (i.e., success probability increase over random guessing) exponentially by requiring the algorithm to predict the XOR of L instances. This is formalized in the following result that we call the learning XOR lemma. 

Lemma E.30 (Learning XOR lemma) . Let N be a large integer. Let T1, L be positive integers. Let 

η ∈ (0 , 2] be a constant and c = (865 /η 2) log ␀865 /η 2. Let g : {0, 1}b × { 0, 1}b → { 0, 1} be a noisy encoding function with encoding length b ≥ c log N and discrepancy disc( g) ≤ 2−ηb . Let f : O → {0, 1}, O ⊆ { 0, 1}N be any function. Suppose that any randomized classical learning algorithm L with space complexity T1L(S+⌈log L⌉), sample complexity T1N , and input form I = [ N ]×{ 0, 1}b×{ 0, 1}, given data generated from DNg,N (o), o ∼ pγ , cannot output γ ∈ { 0, 1} with success probability more than 1/2 + δ.Then, any randomized classical learning algorithm L⊕ with space complexity S, sample complexity T1N ,and input form I × [L], given data generated from (D1(γj ,o j ,Y (0 ,j ),Y (1 ,j ))Lj=1 

→×T1 D2(β,α,Y (α,β )) →×N z) as in dynamic NOPE, cannot output B = LLj=1 γj ∈ { 0, 1} with success probability more than 

12 + (2 δ)L

2 (E85) There are two challenges in proving Theorem E.30. The first challenge is that the random sampling of β

may allow the algorithm to see more samples that belong to the same problem instance labeled by β. For 95 example, if two consecutive β’s are the same, then the algorithm effectively have twice as many samples, and thus have a higher success probability than usual. The second challenge is that the L problem instances are actually not independent, because the algorithm can carry information through the computation and may be able to solve several instances in a joint way. In other words, the algorithm does not have to approach each problem instances independently. The random sampling of β’s, moreover, allows the algorithm to introduce more correlation between problem instances, as compared to a case where streams of different instances are concatenated sequentially in an adversarial order (cf. the streaming XOR lemma as in [99, Theorem 1]). To overcome these challenges, we proceed in two steps to prove Theorem E.30. Firstly, we derandomize β

by showing that any learning algorithm L⊕ can be used to construct a learning algorithm L′ with the same success probability, but increased space complexity S +⌈log L⌉ and sample complexity T1N L , for a different data generation process where β appears in a fixed ordering β = 1 , . . . , L, 1, . . . , L, . . . rather than randomly sampled (there are still N samples under each β). Next, we show that the learning algorithm L′ cannot have success probability more than 1 /2 + (2 δ)L/2 even if it can correlate different instances, by analyzing a corresponding communication problem that takes into account the correlation between different problem instances generated by the algorithm. Together, this shows that the success probability of L⊕ cannot exceed 1 /2 + (2 δ)L/2.

Lemma E.31 (Derandomize β). Let L⊕ be any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity

, outputs B = LLj=1 γj ∈ { 0, 1} with the same probability psucc .Proof of Theorem E.31. We prove this lemma by explicitly constructing the learning algorithm L′ from L⊕. The constructed algorithm L′ process the data in chunks of size N L . At the beginning of each chunk, L′ randomly selects a β ∼ Uniform([ L]) and keeps it in its memory. This uses ⌈log L⌉ bits of memory. It waits until the data block of size N that corresponds to this particular β arrives, and then feeds these N samples sequentially into L⊕, which operates on the remaining memory of size S. After processing these N samples, L′ waits until the end of this N L -size chunk and erases its selection of β. Then it proceeds to the next N L -size chunk and randomly selects a new β. After processing all the T1 chunks of size N L , L′ has already given T1N data samples to L⊕ and L⊕ generates an output bit in return, which is then outputted by L′ as the final outcome. Note that since the β’s are selected uniformly, the data samples that are fed into L⊕ follow exactly the same distribution as ( D1(γj ,o j ,Y (0 ,j ),Y (1 ,j ))Lj=1 

→×T1 D2(β,α,Y (α,β )) →×N z), by definition of Dorder (γj )Lj=1 

in Equation (E86). This implies that the success probability of L′ is the same as that of L⊕.

d. Derandomized learning XOR lemma 

In the second step of proving Theorem E.30, we show that the success probability of L′ cannot exceed 1/2 + (2 δ)L/2. The proof idea follows that of [99, Theorem 1], but the structure of our data generation processes 96 are very different from the adversarial ordering there. Therefore, we adopt a different technical construction. In particular, their data streams are concatenated sequentially whereas ours are interleaved as in Equation (E86). This interleaved structure is inherent in our learning setting, because our problem instance labels β are sampled randomly. 

Lemma E.32 (Derandomized learning XOR lemma) . Suppose that any randomized classical learning algorithm 

L with space complexity T1LS and sample complexity T1N , given data generated from DNg,N (o), o ∼ pγ , cannot output γ ∈ { 0, 1} with success probability more than 1/2 + δ. Then, any randomized classical learning algorithm 

L′ with space complexity S and sample complexity T1N L , given data generated from Dorder (γj )Lj=1 

, cannot output 

B = LLj=1 γj ∈ { 0, 1} with success probability more than 1/2 + (2 δ)L/2.

To prove Theorem E.32, we consider an L-player communication game. In particular, there are L players 

Q1, . . . , Q L where the player Qβ receives the data zβ

π based on the learning algorithm L′, and the communication between different players will characterize the correlation between problem instances induced by the learning algorithm. Without loss of generality, we assume that the learning algorithm L′ and the communication protocol π are both deterministic, since there always exists a way of fixing the random numbers in a randomized algorithm such that the resulting success probability is the same as that of the randomized algorithm. We work in the blackboard model for communication in this game. In other words, the players each write a message on the blackboard in the order Q1, . . . , Q L and the messages written on the blackboard are visible to all players afterwards. This constitutes one round of communication, and the messages are never erased. The next round starts again from Q1. We use Mji to denote the message written by player Qi in round j. Let Bji

be the content of the blackboard before player Qi communicates in round j, and let Bj be the content of the blackboard after round j completes. Suppose that there are r rounds in total. For any β ∈ [L] and blackboard content B, we define bias π (β, B) = | Pr[ γβ = 0 |B r = B] − Pr[ γβ = 1 |B r = B]| (E88) to be the bias of γβ conditioned on the final blackboard displaying B. This intuitively characterizes the progress 

π makes in solving the β-th problem instance. Similarly, we define bias π (B) = Pr

To do this, we need to show that different γβ ’s are independent even when conditioned on the final blackboard content Br , which can be used by 

π to make predictions. We make use of the following fact from information theory. 

Lemma E.34 ([99, Proposition A.4]) . For any random variables A, B, C, D , if A and D are independent conditioned on both B and C, then 

I(A; B|CD ) ≤ I(A; B|C). (E94) 98 

Proof. Note that since A and D are independent conditioned on B, C , we have 

H(A|BCD ) = H(AD |BC ) − H(D|BC ) = H(A|BC ) + H(D|BC ) − H(D|BC ) = H(A|BC ). (E95) Therefore, 

I(A; B|CD ) = H(A|CD ) − H(A|BCD ) = H(A|CD ) − H(A|BC ) ≤ H(A|C) − H(A|BC ) = I(A; B|C), (E96) where we have used the fact that conditioning reduces entropy H(A|CD ) ≤ H(A|C). We use this to prove the following lemma that shows the conditional independence of problem instances. e. Proof of Theorem E.30 

We are now ready to prove the original learning XOR lemma (Theorem E.30). Recall that the general proof idea is the following. We first use the learning algorithm L⊕ to construct an algorithm L′ with the same success probability but learns from the derandomized distribution Dorder (γj )Lj=1 

as in Theorem E.31. Then, we invoke Theorem E.32 to upper bound the success probability of L′, which implies an upper bound the success probability of L⊕.

Proof of Theorem E.30. Let L⊕ be any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity S and sample complexity T1N , such that given data generated from ( D1(γj ,o j ,Y (0 ,j ),Y (1 ,j ))Lj=1 

→×T1 D2(β,α,Y (α,β )) →×N z)as in dynamic NOPE, outputs B = LLj=1 γj ∈ { 0, 1} with success probability psucc . Theorem E.31 implies that there exists a randomized classical learning algorithm L′ with space complexity S+⌈log L⌉ and sample complexity 

T1N L that given data generated from Dorder (γj )Lj=1 

, outputs B = LLj=1 γj ∈ { 0, 1} with probability psucc . Now we invoke Theorem E.32, which shares the assumption of Theorem E.30, and asserts that the success probability of 

L′ is at most 1 /2+(2 δ)L/2. Therefore, we have psucc ≤ 1/2+(2 δ)L/2, concluding the proof of Theorem E.30. 

f. Low advantage leads to large sample complexity 

The learning XOR lemma (Theorem E.30) shows that the advantage of predicting B within a single γ ∈ { 0, 1}L

instance decays exponentially with L. In this section, we translate this exponentially decaying advantage into a blow up in sample complexity using a hybrid argument.

> g. Proof of Theorems E.27 and E.28

We are now ready to prove the classical single-block hardness and sample complexity of dynamic NOPE (Theorems E.27 and E.28). > 5. Connect to applications

In this section, we develop the necessary tools that connect dynamic NOPE to the various application tasks that we will explore in Section F, such as solving linear systems, binary classification, dimension reduction, etc. We begin by focusing on a specific oracle property called Forrelation [95, 96] and take the inner product function as the noisy encoding function. We design a distributional version of Forrelation, show that inner product is a good noisy encoding function, and prove some useful lemmas in Section E 5 a. In Section E 5 b, we construct an efficient quantum circuit that solves the dynamic NOPE task, which we then embed into various applications. In particular, in Sections E 5 c to E 5 e, we embed this circuit into the tasks of solving linear systems, binary classification, and dimension reduction. This shows that if we can solve the application tasks, we can solve dynamic NOPE, which is hard. Hence the application tasks must also be hard to solve. The full hardness proofs will be given in Section F for the various application tasks. 102

However, in most classical data processing applications, we work in real numbers. The following standard lemma provides a canonical way to realify complex matrices and vectors. 

Lemma E.43 (Realification) . For any complex vector ⃗v ∈ C d, we define its realification R[⃗v] ∈ R 2d as 

R[⃗v] =

Then, the following properties hold 1. (Isometry): ∥R [⃗v]∥ 2 = ∥⃗v∥ 2, ∀⃗v ∈ C d.2. (Inner product): R[⃗u] T R[⃗v] = Re[ ⃗u †⃗v], ∀⃗u,⃗ v ∈ C d.3. (Linearity): R[A⃗ v] = R[A]R[⃗v], ∀A ∈ C d×d,⃗ v ∈ C d.4. (Matrix operations): for any A, B ∈ Cd×d, R[AB ] = R[A]R[B], R[A†] = R[A]T . If A is invertible, 

R[A]−1 = R[A−1].105 

5. (Singular values): For any A ∈ Cd×d, the singular values of R[A] are the same as those of A, with doubled multiplicity. Consequently, the norm and condition number of R[A] are the same as those of A.6. (Structured matrices): The realification of Hermitian matrices are symmetric. The realification of unitary matrices are orthogonal. The realification of s-sparse matrices are 2s-sparse. 7. (Eigenvectors): For any Hermitian matrix A ∈ Cd×d with the largest eigenvalue λ and a unique corre-sponding eigenvector ⃗v, its realification R[A] is symmetric with a two-fold degenerate largest eigenvalue λ

corresponding to two eigenvectors R[⃗v], R[i⃗ v].

The above standard realification doubles the eigenvalue degeneracy of a matrix. To avoid that, we use the following realification of quantum circuits [179, 180]. 

Lemma E.44 (Realification of quantum circuits) . Let UT · · · U1, U i ∈ U (2 n) be a sequence of T unitaries on n

qubits. Let |ψ⟩ ∈ C2n

be an n-qubit quantum state. Then, R[Ui], i ∈ [T ] are 2n+1 -dimensional real, orthogonal matrices and therefore unitaries on n + 1 qubits. Similarly, R[|ψ⟩] is a quantum state on n + 1 qubits with real components. In particular, R[|x⟩] = |0⟩ | x⟩ , ∀x ∈ { 0, 1}n.Proof. This follows directly from Items 1 and 6 of Theorem E.43. Theorem E.44 avoids the degeneracy problem because of the simple observation: the eigenspace of R[|ψ⟩⟨ ψ|]with eigenvalue one is (doubly) degenerate, while that of R[|ψ⟩]R[|ψ⟩]T is not.

O : |x, β ⟩ → (−1) (oβ )x |x, β ⟩ , ∀x ∈ [N K ], β ∈ [L]. (E151) with one more ancilla (e.g., in |−⟩ ). As a result, we have constructed the phase oracle for solving any β ∈ [L]instance of the Forrelation problem (see Theorem E.38) using O(1) ancilla and O(b) queries to O0. Note that here we are computing the inner product bit by bit at the cost of an additional factor of b in query complexity, as compared to use O(b) ancilla and compute the inner product directly. We choose this way of computing the inner product to keep the space complexity small: log N + O(log log N ) rather than O(b) = c log N with a large constant c. This will be crucial in determining the size of the linear system in Theorem E.46 and proving the final classical hardness result with the optimal exponent. Now, we invoke Theorem E.40, which gives us a quantum algorithm AO with log N + O(log log(1 /η )) space complexity, O(K210 K log N log(1 /η )) = O(log N log(1 /η )) gate complexity, making O(K210 K log(1 /η )) = 

O(log(1 /η )) queries to O (with β register set to β) that outputs γβ with success probability at least 1 − η,when oβ ∼ pγβ . Using AO , we compute the parity B = LLβ=1 γβ as follows. We introduce an ancilla qubit 

a to record the result. We first initialize the β register to |1⟩, execute AO , and apply a CNOT gate to copy the output qubit to the ancilla a. Then we run AO in reverse to uncompute, add one to β, execute AO again (now for β = 2), and copy out the result with a CNOT gate. We repeat the above procedure until β = L.Finally, we measure the ancilla qubit and output the measurement outcome. The union bound asserts that with probability at least 1 − 2Lη , all 2 L executions so AO and its inverse are simultaneously correct and the output bit is equal to B = LLβ=1 γβ . Note that here we are reusing the ancilla and working space for different 107 executions of AO by uncomputation to save space. We set η = 1 /(20 L). Then we have a quantum circuit A

such that when we measure the first qubit (the ancilla a), we obtain B with success probability at least 0 .9. It has space complexity n1 + O(log log L) = log N + O(log log N ) + O(log log L) = log N + O(log log N ), gate complexity O(L log N log(1 /η )) = O(L log N log log N ) = O(log 3 N log log N ), and queries O0

Q = O(L log L) = O(log 2 N log log N ) (E152) times. Moreover, since we always uncompute every time we execute AO , all the remaining qubits end up in the 

|0⟩ state. This completes the proof of Theorem E.45. 

c. Connect to linear systems

d. Connect to binary classification 

Then, we move on to the application of binary classification. We consider binary classification with least-squares SVM. In a least-squares SVM, we consider a binary-label training dataset of N samples ( ⃗x i, y i)Ni=1 ,each specified by a D-dimensional feature vector ⃗x i ∈ RD and a label yi ∈ {± 1}. We use a matrix X =(⃗x 1, . . . ,⃗ x N )T ∈ RN ×D and a vector ⃗y = ( y 1, . . . , y N )T ∈ {± 1}N to represent the training set. The goal is to use the training set to classify a new test point ⃗x ∈ R D via ˆ y = sgn( ⃗x T (XT X + λI D )−1XT⃗y), where λ ≥ 0 is the ℓ2 regularization strength. We show below that any quantum circuit UT · · · U1 of size T can be embedded into a training dataset ( X,⃗ y)with feature length D = N and a sparse test vector ⃗x such that calculating ˆ y = sgn( ⃗x T (XT X)−1XT⃗y) with no regularization suffices to determine sgn( ⟨ψ| Z1 |ψ⟩), where |ψ⟩ = UT · · · U1 |0⟩ is the output state of the circuit. One can interpret this result as proving the BQP hardness of binary classification. This will be used to prove classical hardness of binary classification in Section F 2.

e. Connect to dimension reduction 

Finally, we consider the application of dimension reduction. We focus on the task of performing principal component analysis (PCA) on a data matrix X ∈ RN ×D , which reduces the data to one dimension. That means finding the eigenvector ⃗w ∈ R D of XT X ∈ RD×D with the largest eigenvalue λmax and projecting the test vector ⃗x ∈ R D to that direction as ⃗x ·⃗w. This reduces the test vector ⃗x to a scalar ⃗x ·⃗w, its 1D representation. In the following, we show that any quantum circuit UT · · · U1 of size T can be embedded into a data matrix 

X with feature length D = N and a sparse test vector ⃗x such that calculating the 1D representation ⃗x ·⃗w to 1/poly( T, log( N )) error suffices to determine the output of that circuit. We also show that there is a sparse guiding vector that have good overlap with the principal component. One can interpret this result as proving the BQP hardness of dimension reduction given a good guiding vector. This will be used to prove the classical hardness of dimension reduction in Section F 3.

## N

FIG. 8: Overview of the linear system task. We illustrate the linear system task with a particular real-world application scenario in power grid analysis. In power grid analysis, samples are obtained by performing measurements on a power grid to read off the impedance values and the voltage values of random locations that are changing dynamically. We may want to estimate the heat dissipation of some critical junctions, given by a quadratic form of the current. This reduces to solving a high-dimensional linear system A⃗ x =⃗ b. Our results show that a small quantum machine with size poly(log N ) can solve this task with ˜O(N ) samples, whereas any classical machine with exponentially larger size O(N 0.99 )cannot solve the task unless it uses a sample size at least super-polynomial in N .

Appendix F: Applications 

In this section, we apply quantum oracle sketching to various applications, including solving linear systems (Section F 1 and Figure 8), binary classification (Section F 2 and Figure 9), and dimension reduction (Sec-tion F 3). We show that we can solve these useful classical data processing tasks in an end-to-end fashion with a small quantum computer. Then, we use techniques developed in Section E to rigorously prove the classical hardness of solving these tasks. In particular, we prove that, using the same number of samples, any classical machine needs exponentially larger size to solve these tasks. Moreover, in a dynamic setting, if the classical ma-chine does not have enough size, it would need super-polynomially more samples. Throughout this section, we use |x⟩ = P

j xj |j⟩ /∥⃗x∥ 2 to denote the real quantum state corresponding to any vector ⃗x = ( x 1, . . . , x N )T ∈ RN .

1. Linear system 

We begin with the fundamental primitive of solving linear systems, summarized in Figure 8. The solution of large linear systems serves as the computational bottleneck in a vast array of scientific and engineering disciplines. It is a key subroutine in many problems including regression, optimization, and solving differential equations that governs chemicals, materials, biological systems, fluid, and mechanical systems, etc. As we increase the dataset size, sampling rate, or precision, the dimension N of these systems grows rapidly. Consequently, storing the data for processing becomes a bottleneck. In this section, we show that a small quantum machine can solve linear systems better than exponentially larger classical machines. Formally, we consider N -dimensional linear systems of the form 

A⃗ x =⃗ b, A ∈ RN ×N ,⃗ x,⃗ b ∈ RN , (F1) where A is a symmetric, sparse and well-conditioned matrix with operator norm ∥A∥ ≤ 1 after appropriate rescaling. The assumption that A is symmetric incurs no loss of generality; any general linear system can always be embedded into a symmetric formulation Asym ⃗x sym =⃗ bsym , where Asym =

 0 AAT 0



∈ R2N ×2N and ⃗bsym = ( ⃗b,⃗ 0) T ∈ R2N with the solution ⃗x sym = ( ⃗0,⃗ x) T . This embedding preserves the sparsity and condition number. We characterize the tractability of the linear system with two parameters: the sparsity s (the maximal number of nonzero elements per row or column) and condition number κ = ∥A−1∥∥ A∥. We focus on the regime where 115 the dimension N is very large, and yet it remains sparse and well-conditioned: 

s, κ ≤ poly(log N ). (F2)  

> a. Problem formulation

Now we formally define our data processing task of solving linear systems, summarized in Theorems F.1 and F.2. In particular, we specify the data generation process and the goal of the task. We consider a data generation process where we randomly get a non-zero matrix element of coefficient matrix A and a random component of the bias vector ⃗ b = ( b1, . . . , b N )T .Recall that in Section D 5, we define the matrix data generation process of the matrix A as a hierarchical data generation process that generates random non-zero matrix elements as data 

zcoeff = ( i, j, A ij ), (i, j ) marginal 

∼ Uniform( {(i, j ) : Aij ̸ = 0 }). (F3) We assume that the linear system is properly normalized such that ∥A∥ ≤ 1. The vector data generation process of ⃗ b is a hierarchical data generation process that generates random components of the vector as data 

zbias = ( k, b k), k marginal 

∼ Uniform([ N ]) . (F4) The matrix elements Aij and vector components bk are specified by bitstrings of length b = poly(log N ) to sufficient accuracy. For simplicity, we assume that these binary representations are exact and use Aij , b k to stand for the corresponding values. In the task of solving linear systems, we consider any hierarchical data generation process DLS (A,⃗ b ) with bounded repetition number R that generates data samples of the form 

z = ( zcoeff , z bias ), (F5) where 

zcoeff = ( i, j, A ij ), (i, j ) marginal 

∼ Uniform( {(i, j ) : Aij ̸ = 0 }), zbias = ( k, b k), k marginal 

∼ Uniform([ N ]) (F6) gives us random non-zero matrix elements of the coefficient matrix A and random components of the bias vector ⃗b. Recall that the repetition number R characterizes the correlation in the data, and is defined as 

R = max  

> z

(E [Nz |z1 = z] − E[Nz ]) , (F7) where Nz = Pτi=1 δzi,z is the number of repeating z’s in a refreshing block of data and τ is refreshing time that bounds the correlation time scale. The specific way of sampling these data can be arbitrary as long as it satisfies the above form. Our discussion generalizes straightforwardly to the alternative scenario where each time we can choose to get either zcoeff or zbias .Next, we specify the goal of solving a high-dimensional linear system. The standard task of calculating the whole solution vector ⃗x = A −1⃗ b is undesirable when the dimension is very large, since writing down the solution vector already takes O(N ) memory. Instead, in many cases, our goal is to estimate some property of the solution vector. In particular, we consider the prototypical task of estimating the normalized quadratic form value ⃗x T M⃗x

∥⃗x∥∥M∥∥ ⃗x∥ (F8) to ϵ additive error for some symmetric matrix M ∈ RN ×N with known norm ∥M∥ . We assume that M can be efficiently measured on a quantum machine. That means the space and time complexity of measuring M are both poly(log N ). This assumption can be relaxed if we allow the quantum machine to run longer. This motivates our definition of the linear system task as follows. 116 

Task F.1 (Linear system task) . Let N, R be integers and ϵ ∈ (0 , 1] . Consider any symmetric matrix 

M ∈ RN ×N that specifies a quadratic form of our interest and can be efficiently measured. The linear system task is to estimate the normalized quadratic form value ⃗x T M⃗x

∥⃗x∥∥M∥∥ ⃗x∥ (F9) 

to ϵ additive error using data samples from any data generation process DLS (A,⃗ b ) defined above with repetition number at most R that corresponds to a linear system A⃗ x =⃗ b, A ∈ RN ×N ,⃗ b ∈ RN , where the coefficient matrix A has poly(log N ) sparsity and poly(log N ) condition number. 

Our quantum algorithm is flexible enough to handle correlated data with time-varying features. This allows us to further consider a dynamic scenario, where the linear system changes over time, but the property we want to estimate remains approximately fixed. This resembles the batch processing strategy common in modern large-scale data analysis, where we keep processing batches of data that share a common underlying property that we want to learn. We use θ ∈ R to denote that underlying property value. In particular, we consider any hierarchical data generation process DDLS (θ, ϵ ) with bounded repetition number R and refreshing time τ of the form 

DDLS (θ, ϵ ) = ( D0

→ D LS (A,⃗ b ) →×τ z). (F10) In other words, the linear system A⃗ x =⃗ b changes every τ time steps and we keep getting random non-zero matrix elements of A and random components of ⃗ b of the current linear system as the data. We require all linear systems sampled from D0 

> θ,ϵ

to have poly(log N ) sparsity, poly(log N ) condition number, and the desired property: ⃗x T M⃗x

∥⃗x∥∥M∥∥ ⃗x∥ − θ ≤ ϵ,⃗ x = A −1⃗ b. (F11) The specific way that D0

samples the linear system can be arbitrary as long as it satisfies the above requirements. Formally, we define the dynamic linear system task as follows. 

Task F.2 (Dynamic linear system task) . Let N, R, τ be integers and ϵ ∈ (0 , 1] . Consider any symmetric matrix M ∈ RN ×N that specifies a quadratic form of our interest and can be efficiently measured. The dynamic linear system task is to estimate the underlying normalized quadratic form value θ to 2ϵ

additive error using data samples from any DDLS (θ, ϵ ) defined above with repetition number at most R

and refreshing time τ .

In the following, we first state our main results on quantum advantage in solving linear systems. Then we prove the quantum easiness and classical hardness in subsequent sections.  

> b. Main results

Our first result shows that given the same amount of samples, a small quantum machine can solve the linear system task better than an exponentially larger classical machine. Note that the scaling N 1−ζ is effectively N

since it holds for any constant ζ > 0. 

Theorem F.3 (Quantum advantage in solving linear systems) . Consider the linear system task with dimension N and repetition number R defined in Theorem F.1. Using ˜O(RN ) samples, a quantum machine with poly(log N ) size can solve it with 1/poly(log N ) error and high success probability, while any classical machine with o(N 1−ζ ) size for any constant ζ > 0 cannot solve it with constant error and success probability more than 1/2 + 1 /N ω(1) . Moreover, the data processing time per sample of the quantum machine is poly(log N ).

The second result shows that if the size of the classical machine is slightly smaller than o(N ), it would need super-polynomially more samples than a small quantum machine to solve the dynamic linear system task. In the context of batch processing, this means that quantum machines can solve the task with a few batches of data, whereas sub-exponential size classical machines require super-polynomially many batches. 117 

Theorem F.4 (Quantum advantage in solving dynamic linear systems) . Consider the dynamic linear system task with dimension N , repetition number R, and sufficient refreshing time τ = ˜O(RN ) defined in Theorem F.2. A quantum machine with poly(log N ) size can use ˜O(RN ) samples to solve it with 

1/poly(log N ) error and high success probability, while any classical machine with o(N 1−ζ ) size for any constant ζ > 0 that solves it with constant error and probability at least 2/3 must collect at least RN ω(1) 

samples. Moreover, the data processing time per sample of the quantum machine is poly(log N ).

Together, Theorems F.3 and F.4 establish unconditional and exponential quantum advantages in the founda-tional task of solving linear systems that appears in almost every aspect of science and engineering.  

> c. Quantum algorithm

Here, we prove the quantum algorithm parts of Theorems F.3 and F.4. We construct an algorithm that solves a high-dimensional linear system on a small quantum computer. We do so by combining quantum oracle sketching (Theorem D.16), quantum state sketching (Theorem D.24), and standard quantum linear system solvers. We use the Heisenberg-scaling quantum amplitude estimation to measure M, which gives us the optimal 1 /ϵ 2 sample complexity after the quadratic slowdown. One may also use standard quantum limit measurements that will lead to a 1 /ϵ 4 dependence on the error ϵ.

Theorem F.5 (Solving linear systems with quantum oracle sketching) . Let ϵ, δ ∈ (0 , 1) . Let N be a large integer and A⃗ x =⃗ b be an N -dimensional linear system. Here, A ∈ RN ×N is an s-sparse matrix with norm ∥A∥ ≤ 1 and condition number at most κ.⃗ x,⃗ b ∈ RN are N -dimensional vectors. Consider any symmetric matrix M ∈ RN ×N that specifies a quadratic form of our interest and can be efficiently measured using SM qubits. Then, there exists a quantum algorithm that produces an estimate ˆθ ∈ [−1, 1] 

satisfying

We note that Theorem F.5 immediately implies the quantum algorithm part of Theorem F.3 by definition of the linear system task. It also proves the quantum algorithm part of Theorem F.4 by taking τ = ˜O(RN ) larger than M = ˜O(RN ) and noting that

We use the following quantum linear system solver as the backbone of our quantum algorithm and instantiate the oracle queries with quantum oracle sketching. We refer interested readers to a recent survey [181] for an overview on alternative quantum linear system solvers. 118 

Lemma F.6 (Quantum linear system solver [82]) . Let n be an integer and N = 2 n. Let A⃗ x =⃗ b be a linear system, where A ∈ CN ×N ,⃗ x,⃗ b ∈ CN with ∥A∥ ≤ 1 and condition number at most κ. There exists a quantum algorithm that produces a state ρ satisfying 

∥ρ − | x⟩⟨ x| ∥ 1 ≤ ϵ, |x⟩ = A−1 |b⟩∥A−1 |b⟩ ∥ , (F16) 

using O(n) qubits, O(κ log(1 /ϵ )) gates, and O(κ log(1 /ϵ )) queries to the block encoding of A and the state preparation unitary of |b⟩ and their inverse and controlled versions. 

Recall that in Theorem D.23, we have shown that ˜O(RN s 5) samples suffice to implement the block encoding of an s-sparse matrix A. In addition, Theorem D.24 shows that we can use ˜O(RN ) samples to prepare the quantum state |b⟩ = PNj=1 bj |j⟩ /∥⃗b∥ of the vector ⃗ b = ( b1, . . . , b N )T . Together, these primitives allow us to implement all the necessary subroutines that we need in quantum linear system solvers. In particular, we combine Theorem F.6 with Theorem D.23 and Theorem D.24 to prove Theorem F.5. 

Proof of Theorem F.5. Let n = ⌈log 2(N )⌉ and embed A,⃗ b into 2 n ∈ [N, 2N ] dimension to apply quantum oracle and state sketching. Theorem F.6 states that there is a quantum linear system solver that produces a state ρ

We first use this quantum linear system solver to construct a query algorithm that estimates the quadratic form value to ϵ error with probability at least 1 − δ/ 2. Then, we instantiate the queries with quantum oracle and state sketching. The instantiation error is chosen to be δ/ 2 such that the total variation distance error on the final estimate is δ/ 2. This immediately implies that the final estimate is accurate up to ϵ error with probability at least 1 − δ/ 2 − δ/ 2 = 1 − δ.As the first step, we construct the query algorithm that estimates the quadratic form value. > d. Classical hardness

In this section, we prove the two classical hardness results in Theorems F.3 and F.4. We prove them by constructing a specific linear system using Theorem E.46. Solving it amounts to solving the (dynamic) Noisy Oracle Property Estimation (NOPE) task defined in Section E (Theorems E.1 and E.26), whose classical hardness we have already proved in Theorems E.27 and E.28. This gives us two classical hardness results. The first result follows from Theorem E.27 and shows that any classical learning algorithm that wants to perform better than random guessing in solving a linear system with the same number of samples quantum algorithms need must have Ω( N 1−ζ ) size for any constant ζ > 0. The second result follows from Theorem E.28 and shows that when the linear system is dynamic, any classical learning algorithm will need a number of samples super-polynomial in N if it has size o(N 1−ζ ) for any constant 

ζ > 0. The general idea of proving these results is to embed the quantum circuit that solves dynamic NOPE from Theorem E.45 into a linear system via Theorem E.46. The normalized quadratic form value ⃗ x T M⃗x 

> ∥⃗x∥∥M∥∥ ⃗x∥

encodes the oracle property B ∈ { 0, 1} that we want to estimate in (dynamic) NOPE. Then we show that given any classical algorithm that solves the linear system task of this specific linear system, we can use it to construct a classical algorithm that solves (dynamic) NOPE. In the (dynamic) NOPE task, we take the oracle property 120 function f to be K-Forrelation (Theorem E.38) and the noisy encoding function g to be the inner product (Theorem E.41). In the following, we construct the data generation process DN,K,R

is an integer that specifies the repetition number, and B indicates whether the quadratic form of the solution vector has a large or small value (see Equation (E158)). The goal is to solve for B. It helps to compare this construction to the dynamic NOPE data generation process defined in Section E 4. Given N , we define another large integer N ′ as follows. Let T = Θ(log 3 N ′ log log N ′) be the total number of two-qubit gates and diagonal gates in the quantum circuit from Theorem E.45. FIG. 9: Overview of the binary classification task. We illustrate the binary classification task with a particular real-world application scenario in sentiment analysis of movie reviews. We collect positive and negative reviews of movies from users, encode them into feature vectors ⃗x i ∈ RD , and use them to predict labels of new samples. A sample is the feature vector of a review ⃗x i and its label yi (positive or negative). A canonical way of binary classification is the support vector machine (SVM), which is trained on the high-dimensional data matrix X ∈ RN ×D and label vector ⃗y and produces a decision boundary represented by a weight vector ⃗w ∈ R D that classifies new samples. Our results show that a small quantum machine with size poly(log D) can solve this task with ˜O(N ) samples, whereas any classical machine with exponentially larger size O(D0.99 ) cannot solve the task unless it uses a sample size at least super-polynomial in N .

2. Binary classification 

In this section, we consider the fundamental task of classification in data science and machine learning. We focus on the prototypical example of binary classification. In a binary classification task, we are given N data samples of the form 

zi = ( i,⃗ x i, y i),⃗ x i ∈ RD , y i ∈ {± 1}, i ∈ [N ], (F42) where ⃗x i is a vector of D features and yi ∈ {± 1} is its binary label. This forms a training dataset of size N ,represented by 

X = ( ⃗x 1, . . . ,⃗ x N )T =



—⃗x T

1 —...—⃗x TN —

 ∈ RN ×D ,⃗ y =



y1

...

yN

 ∈ {± 1}N , (F43) where X is the feature matrix and ⃗y is the label vector. Here, we adopt the convention used in data science, where each row represents a data sample and each column is a feature. The goal of binary classification is to use this training dataset to predict the labels of future unseen test data samples ⃗x ′.A standard method for performing binary classification is support vector machine (SVM). The most basic version of SVM is the least-squares SVM (LS-SVM), where we aim to find a weight vector ⃗w ∈ R D that explains the dataset by minimizing the quadratic loss function min ⃗w∈R D

∥X⃗ w −⃗y∥ 22 + λ∥⃗w∥ 22 (F44) with ℓ2 regularization strength λ ≥ 0. This method is also known as ridge classifier. Generalizations to other regularization methods such as ℓ1 (lasso) are straightforward. We assume that the data matrix has been properly normalized such that ∥X∥ ≤ 1 and all matrix elements |Xij | ≤ 1. The closed form solution of the weight vector reads ⃗w = ( X T X + λI D )−1XT⃗y, (F45) where ID is the D × D identity matrix. We predict the label of a test vector ⃗x ′ according to the rule ˆy = sgn( ⃗x ′ ·⃗w) = sgn( ⃗x ′T (XT X + λI D )−1XT⃗y). (F46) 124 We assume that the test vector is also properly normalized such that all its components have magnitude less than one: ∥⃗x ′∥∞ ≤ 1. The classifiability of a test vector ⃗x ′ can be characterized by the margin γ(⃗x ′), defined as 

γ(⃗x ′) = |⃗x ′ ·⃗w|∥⃗w∥ 2

, (F47) which is the magnitude of the projection of ⃗x ′ on to the vector ⃗w, indicating how far the test point is from the decision boundary. A classifiable test vector should have a margin that is not too small (e.g., γ(⃗x ′) ≥

1/poly(log N, log D)). We characterize the tractability of the binary classification task by three parameters: the sparsity s, the regularized condition number κreg , and the classifiability of the test vector γ(⃗x ′). The sparsity s is the maximal number of non-zero elements in X per row or column, which bounds the number of non-zero features per sample and the number of samples each feature is presented in. Such sparsity appears in many natural datasets (e.g., natural language data with common words trimmed off.) The regularized condition number is the condition number of the matrix that we invert in LS-SVM, XT X + λI D , which is equal to 

κreg =

s

σ2max (X) + λσ2min (X) + λ ≤ min 

(

κ, 

r

1 + 1

λ

)

, (F48) where σmax (X), σ min (X) are the maximal and minimal singular value of X and κ = σmax (X)/σ min (X) is the condition number of X. When no regularization is imposed ( λ = 0), the regularized condition number κreg is the same as the condition number κ. In natural dataset, often only the largest few singular values matter for classification, but they are buried in a long tail of small singular values that are not important. Choosing a regularization strength λ that effectively truncates the long tail provides a balance between good regularized condition number κreg and good classification performance. We focus on the regime where the number of samples 

N and feature dimension D are very large, and yet the binary classification task remains sparse, well-conditioned, and classifiable: 

s, κ reg ≤ poly(log N, log D), γ(⃗x ′ 

> j

> j

, j = 1 , . . . m . We call such test vectors classifiable vectors. Note that we always have 

D = ˜Θ( N ) when the sparsity s ≤ poly(log N, log D).  

> a. Problem formulation

Now we formally define our data processing task of binary classification, summarized in Theorems F.9 and F.10. In particular, we specify the data generation process and the goal of the task. We consider a data generation process where we randomly get a sparse feature vector ( i,⃗ x i) from the sparse feature matrix X

and its corresponding label yi. The components of ⃗x i are specified by bitstrings of length b = poly(log N, log D)to sufficient accuracy. For simplicity, we assume that this binary representation is exact and use ⃗x i to stand for the corresponding values. Specifically, we consider any hierarchical data generation process DBC (X,⃗ y) with bounded repetition number 

R that generates data samples of the form 

z = ( i,⃗ x i, y i), i marginal 

∼ Uniform([ N ]) , (F50) where ⃗x i is a random entry of feature vector in the feature matrix X and yi is the label of that training data point. Recall that the repetition number R characterize the correlation in the data, and is defined as 

R = max

The specific way of sampling these data can be arbitrary as long as it satisfies the above form. Our results generalize straightforwardly to the more demanding scenario, where we only get a random non-zero subset of the feature vector ⃗x i, or a random and possibly unmatched label ( j, y j ). Task F.9 (Binary classification task) . Let N, D, R be integers and λ ≥ 0 be any ℓ2 regularization strength. The binary classification task is to predict the label ˆyj of any poly(log N, log D)-sparse and classifiable test vector ⃗x ′

Our quantum algorithm is flexible enough to handle correlated data with time-varying features. This allow us to further consider a dynamic scenario, where the training data ( X,⃗ y) changes over time, but the labels of the test vectors remain fixed. This resembles the batch training strategy common in modern large-scale machine learning, where we train our model on batches of data that are different but the desired labels of the test vectors are fixed. We use {ˆyj }mj=1 to denote that fixed set of labels of the test vectors ⃗x ′

Task F.10 (Dynamic binary classification task) . Let N, D, R, τ be integers and λ ≥ 0 be any ℓ2 regular-ization strength. The dynamic binary classification task is to predict the label ˆyj of any poly(log N, log D)-sparse and classifiable test vector ⃗x ′

In the following, we first state our main results on quantum advantage in binary classification. Then we prove the quantum easiness and classical hardness in subsequent sections.  

> b. Main results

Our first result shows that given the same amount of samples, a small quantum machine can solve the binary classification task better than an exponentially larger classical machine. This means that using a quantum machine, we can build a better model with exponentially smaller size. Note that the scaling D1−ζ is effectively 

D since it holds for any constant ζ > 0. 

Theorem F.11 (Quantum advantage in binary classification) . Consider the binary classification task with sample dimension N , feature dimension D and repetition number R defined in Theorem F.9. Using 

˜O(RN ) samples, a quantum machine with poly(log D) size can solve it with high success probability, while any classical machine with o(D1−ζ ) size for any constant ζ > 0 cannot solve it with success probability more than 1/2 + 1 /N ω(1) . Moreover, the data processing time per sample of the quantum machine is 

poly(log D).126 The second result shows that if the size of the classical machine is slightly smaller than o(D), it would need super-polynomially more samples than a small quantum machine to solve the dynamic binary classification task. In the context of batch training, this means that quantum machines can solve the task with a few batches of training data, whereas sub-exponential size classical machines require super-polynomially many batches. 

Theorem F.12 (Quantum advantage in dynamic binary classification) . Consider the dynamic binary classification task with sample dimension N , feature dimension D, repetition number R, and sufficient refreshing time τ = ˜O(RN ) defined in Theorem F.10. A quantum machine with poly(log D) size can use 

˜O(RN ) samples to solve it with high success probability, while any classical machine with o(D1−ζ ) size for any constant ζ > 0 that solves it with probability at least 2/3 must collect at least RN ω(1) samples. Moreover, the data processing time per sample of the quantum machine is poly(log D).

Together, Theorems F.11 and F.12 establish unconditional and exponential quantum advantages in the foun-dational task of binary classification.  

> c. Quantum algorithm

Here, we prove the quantum algorithm parts of Theorems F.11 and F.12. We construct an algorithm that runs high-dimensional SVM on a small quantum computer. We do so by combining quantum oracle sketching (Theorem D.16), quantum state sketching (Theorem D.24), and standard quantum ridge regression solver that prepares the weight vector |w⟩ of the LS-SVM. We use a variant of Clifford classical shadow [74], which we call interferometric classical shadow (Theorem F.16), to readout the weight vector |w⟩ into a classical representation, which is used for predicting the labels of all possible test vectors. 

Theorem F.13 (Binary classification with quantum oracle sketching) . Let N, D be large integers, δ ∈

(0 , 1) , and λ ≥ 0 be the ℓ2 regularization strength. Let X = ( ⃗x 1, . . . ,⃗ x N )T ∈ RN ×D ,⃗ y = ( y 1, . . . , y N )T ∈

RN be a training dataset where X is s-sparse with norm ∥X∥ ≤ 1 and regularized condition number 

κreg = p(σ2max (X) + λ)/(σ2min (X) + λ). Consider a set of s-sparse test vectors {⃗x ′ 

> j

}mj=1 , ∥⃗x ′ 

> j

We note that Theorem F.13 immediately implies the quantum algorithm part of Theorem F.11 by definition of the binary classification task. It also proves the quantum algorithm part of Theorem F.12 by taking τ = ˜O(RN )larger than M = ˜O(RN ). We use the following quantum ridge regression solver as the backbone of our quantum algorithm and instantiate the oracle queries with quantum oracle sketching. We note that for coherent usage, the failure probability originally stated in [83] is absorbed into the error parameter ϵ by replacing standard amplitude amplification with fixed-point amplitude amplification. We bound the error in 2-norm to avoid ambiguity in the global phase. The complexity scaling with κreg may be further improved by applying Theorem F.6 to the augmented matrix 127

To make predictions on the labels, we need to estimate the sign of ⟨x|w⟩. Measuring the observable |x⟩⟨ x| on 

|w⟩ only gives us the magnitude | ⟨ x|w⟩ | 2. The standard way of keeping the sign information is via Hadamard test, in which we introduce an ancilla, prepare the state 1√2 (|0⟩ | x⟩ + |1⟩ | w⟩), and measure the ancilla. However, this contradicts our goal of classical shadow, where we want to collect a universal set of data from |w⟩ that can be used to predict many different |x⟩ afterwards. To address this issue, we develop the following technique that we call interferometric classical shadow. 

Lemma F.16 (Interferometric classical shadow) . Let |w⟩ , |x1⟩ , . . . , |xm⟩ ∈ CD be D-dimensional pure states. Assume that we have complete classical descriptions of |x1⟩ , . . . , |xm⟩. Let ϵ, δ ∈ (0 , 1) . Then, there is a quantum algorithm that can predict ˆoi such that

> d. Classical hardness

In this section, we prove the two classical hardness results in Theorems F.11 and F.12. We fix the ℓ2

regularization strength λ = 0 throughout the proof so that the regularized condition number is the same as the condition number of X: κreg = κ. We will construct a (dynamic) binary classification task with N = D.Hence, for simplicity, we will use N alone throughout this section. We prove the classical hardness results by constructing a specific binary classification task using Theorem E.47. Solving it amounts to solving the (dynamic) Noisy Oracle Property Estimation (NOPE) task defined in Section E (Theorems E.1 and E.26), whose classical hardness we have already proved in Theorems E.27 and E.28. This gives us two classical hardness results. The first result follows from Theorem E.27 and shows that any classical learning algorithm that wants to perform better than random guessing in binary classification with the same number of samples quantum algorithms need must have Ω( D1−ζ ) size for any constant ζ > 0. The second result follows from Theorem E.28 and shows that when the binary classification task is dynamic, any classical learning algorithm will need a number of samples super-polynomial in N if it has size o(D1−ζ ) for any constant 

ζ > 0. The general idea of proving these results is to embed the quantum circuit that solves dynamic NOPE from Theorem E.45 into a binary classification task via Theorem E.47. The label ˆ y of a fixed, sparse test vector ⃗x

encodes the oracle property B ∈ { 0, 1} that we want to estimate in (dynamic) NOPE. Then we show that given any classical algorithm that predicts the label ˆ y, we can use it to construct a classical algorithm that solves (dynamic) NOPE. In the (dynamic) NOPE task, we take the oracle property function f to be K-Forrelation (Theorem E.38) and the noisy encoding function g to be the inner product (Theorem E.41). In the following, we construct the data generation process DN,K,R  

> BC

(B), B ∈ { 0, 1} for binary classification, where N is both the sample dimension and the feature dimension (i.e., D = N ), K = Θ(1) is the constant in the Forrelation that we will embed, R is an integer that specifies the repetition number, and B indicates whether the label of that fixed test vector is +1 or −1 (see Theorem E.47). The goal is to solve for B. It helps to compare this construction to the dynamic NOPE data generation process defined in Section E 4. Given N , we define another large integer N ′ as follows. Let T = Θ(log 3 N ′ log log N ′) be the total number of two-qubit gates and diagonal gates given in Theorem E.45 and let n′ = log N ′ + O(log log N ′) be the number of qubits in Theorem E.45. To properly embed that circuit into the N -dimensional training dataset, we define

In particular, we define D2(α,β ) in the following way. We first sample a random row of the matrix X as follows. We note that after uniformly sampling the realification blocks given in Theorem E.43, the linear space corresponding to the matrix X has a particular factorization given by Theorem E.47. We sample the nested blocks of X uniformly randomly. Then we are left with the subspace |t⟩ | ψ⟩ where |t⟩ is the clock register and 

|ψ⟩ is the n′-qubit register that the quantum circuit runs on. We sample a clock time t ∼ Uniform([ T ]) and the matrix is reduced to a specific gate in the n′-qubit subspace (either a fixed two qubit gate or a diagonal gate that depends on Y (α,β )). The remaining subspace further factorizes into |x, α, β, k ⟩ and the rest of the working qubits. We sample a random basis of this n′-qubit subspace by plug in the specific ( α, β ) that we have already sampled, sample x ∼ Uniform([ KN ′]) , k ∼ Uniform([ b]), and sample a computational basis of the rest of the working qubits uniformly random. This together specifies and thus samples a row i of the matrix X. Note that the marginal distribution of i is uniform over [ N ] (because α, β are sampled uniformly), though there are correlations between consecutive samples of i since they share the same set of ( α, β ). Note that by construction of the matrix X as in Theorem E.47, the picked out row vector ⃗x i is O(1) sparse, and has components that is the real or imaginary part of either 1, from the identity matrices in Equations (E154) and (E155), or a matrix element of a fixed two qubit gates, or 1 − (−1) (Y (α,β )

Theorem F.17 (Classical hardness of binary classification) . Let ζ > 0 be any constant. Let N, D be the sample and feature dimension of a binary classification task and R be its repetition number. Using 

˜O(RN ) samples, any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity 

S ≤ o(D1−ζ ) (F90) 

cannot solve the binary classification task with success probability more than 1/2 + 1 /N ω(1) .132 

Theorem F.18 (Classical hardness of dynamic binary classification) . Let ζ > 0 be any constant. Let 

N, D be the sample and feature dimension of a dynamic binary classification task and R, τ = ˜O(RN ) be its repetition number and refreshing time. Any randomized classical learning algorithm that solves the task with success probability at least 2/3 must have sample complexity 

M ≥ RN ω(1) (F91) 

if its space complexity 

S ≤ o(D1−ζ ). (F92) Theorem F.17 immediately implies the classical hardness part of Theorem F.11 because the first ˜O(RN )samples from the constructed binary classification data belongs to the same training dataset and therefore is a valid sequence of non-dynamic binary classification data. Theorem F.18 directly implies the classical hardness part of Theorem F.12. Together with the quantum algorithm result Theorem F.13, this completes the proof of the quantum advantage claims in Theorems F.11 and F.12.

FIG. 10: Overview of the dimension reduction task. We illustrate the dimension reduction task with a particular real-world application scenario in single cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq). We conduct RNA sequencing experiments to obtain gene sequences in cell samples, which are represented as feature vectors ⃗x i ∈ RD (e.g., via frequency counting of k-mer representations). A sample is the feature vector ⃗x i of a single RNA sequence. A canonical way of dimension reduction is principal component analysis (PCA), which is performed on the high-dimensional data matrix X ∈ RN ×D

and produces a principal component vector ⃗w ∈ R D that represents the most important feature combination. We obtain the low dimension representation ξ(⃗x) of a sample ⃗x by projecting it onto the principal component ⃗w. Our results show that a small quantum machine with size poly(log D) can solve this task with ˜O(N ) samples, whereas any classical machine with exponentially larger size O(D0.99 ) cannot solve the task unless it uses a sample size at least super-polynomial in N .

3. Dimension reduction 

In this section, we consider the fundamental task of dimension reduction in data science and machine learning. We focus on principal component analysis (PCA), the standard method for dimension reduction. For simplicity, we consider the task of reducing the dimension to one. It is straightforward to generalize this to more dimensions. In PCA, we are given a dataset X = ( ⃗x 1, . . . ,⃗ x N )T of size N , where each data sample ⃗x i ∈ RD is a D-dimensional feature vector and is properly normalized such that ∥X∥ ≤ 1. The principal component of this dataset X is a unit vector ⃗w ∈ R D , the direction of which explains the most variance of the data. It is the top eigenvector of the covariance matrix XT X ∈ RD×D with the largest eigenvalue λmax = σ2max , where σmax 

is the largest singular value of X. The principal component ⃗w provides us with a way to perform dimension reduction on any set of test vector {⃗x ′

j }mj=1 . We reduce each test vector ⃗x ′

j to one dimension by projecting it to the direction of ⃗w:

ξ(⃗x ′

j ) = ⃗x ′

j ·⃗w,⃗ w = argmax 

∥⃗w∥ 2=1 ⃗ w T XT X⃗ w. (F100) We call ξ(⃗x ′

j ) the one-dimensional (1 D) representation of the test vector ⃗x ′

j . The goal of dimension reduction is to find the 1D representation ξ(⃗x ′

j ) of any test vector ⃗x ′

j , given the dataset X.It is often the case that we can obtain a good initial guess ⃗g ∈ R D , ∥⃗g∥ 2 = 1 , of the principal component. We call this initial guess ⃗g the guiding vector. The quality of a guiding vector ⃗g is given by the overlap between ⃗g

and the true principal component ⃗w:⃗g ·⃗w, (F101) and a good guiding vector should have a large overlap with the principal component, as compared to a random vector that has overlap ∼ 1/√D. For example, when there is a prominent feature in the dataset, the corre-sponding basis vector is usually a good guiding vector. Another way to obtain a good guiding vector, is to first conduct a small-scale PCA on a sub-sampled dataset, and use the resulting principal component as the guiding vector for the full PCA. We characterize the tractability of the dimension reduction task by three parameters: the sparsity s, the spectral gap ∆, and the quality χ of the guiding vector ⃗g defined below. The sparsity s is the maximal number of non-zero elements in X per row or column, which bounds the number of non-zero features per sample and the number of samples each feature is presented in. Such sparsity appears in many natural datasets (e.g., natural 135 language data with common words trimmed off.) The spectral gap ∆ is the gap between the largest eigenvalue 

λmax (XT X) of XT X and the second largest eigenvalue λsec (XT X)∆ = λmax (XT X) − λsec (XT X) = σ2max (X) − σ2sec (X), (F102) where σmax (X), σ sec (X) are the largest and second largest singular values of X. ∆ captures the gap between the signal (the principal component) and the noise (the other singular vectors) in the data. A large gap means a distinguishable signal in the data. This corresponds to the spike in the spiked covariance model, a standard model for the covariance structure of high-dimensional data [183]. Under the power law distribution of eigenvalues usually seen in realistic datasets [81], with the normalization ∥X∥ = 1, the gap is a constant 1 − 2−α = O(1), where α is the exponent in the power law. Even when the spectrum is indeed continuous, the parameter ∆ serves as a cutoff scale at which we wish to distinguish the principal component from competing modes. Our algorithm will return a vector ⃗w that is a linear combination of all singular vectors that have singular values close to the principal component within ∆. These singular vectors are roughly equally well up to precision ∆. Hence this is sufficient for the purpose of dimension reduction. The quality of the guiding vector χ represents how good our initial guess is. We define the quality parameter χ by ⃗g ·⃗w = ˜Θ

 1

D(1 −χ)/2



, (F103) where χ = 0 means that the guiding vector is no better than a random guess, and χ = 1 means that the guiding vector already has constant overlap with the principal component. We assume that χ = Θ(1) is a constant. We focus on the regime where the number of samples N and feature dimension D are very large, and yet the dimension reduction task remains sparse, gapped, and well-guided: 

s ≤ poly(log N, log D), ∆ ≥ 1poly(log N, log D) ,⃗ g ·⃗w ≥ ˜Ω

 1

D(1 −χ)/2



. (F104) Note that we always have D = ˜Θ( N ) when the sparsity s ≤ poly(log N, log D).  

> a. Problem formulation

Now we formally define our data processing task of dimension reduction, summarized in Theorems F.19 and F.20. In particular, we specify the data generation process and the goal of the task. We assume that a guiding vector ⃗g with quality χ is known to us and the state |g⟩ can be prepared in poly(log D) time (e.g., say it is sparse and we can directly prepare the state |g⟩). We consider a data generation process where we randomly get a sparse feature vector ( i,⃗ x i) from the sparse feature matrix X. The components of ⃗x i are specified by bitstrings of length b = poly(log N, log D) to sufficient accuracy. For simplicity, we assume that this binary representation is exact and use ⃗x i to stand for the corresponding values. Specifically, we consider any hierarchical data generation process DDR (X) with bounded repetition number

The specific way of sampling these data can be arbitrary as long as it satisfies the above form. Our results generalize straightforwardly to the more demanding scenario, where we only get a random non-zero subset of the feature vector ⃗x i.Next, we specify the goal of the dimension reduction task. In the following, we first state our main results on quantum advantage in dimension reduction. Then we prove the quantum easiness and classical hardness in subsequent sections.  

> b. Main results

Our first result shows that given the same amount of samples, a small quantum machine can solve the dimension reduction task better than an exponentially larger classical machine. Moreover, the better the quality of the guiding vector is, the larger the quantum advantage is. This means that using a quantum machine, we can build a better model with exponentially smaller size, as long as there is a guiding vector slightly better than random guessing. Note that the scaling D(1 −ζ)χ is effectively Dχ since it holds for any constant ζ > 0. 

Theorem F.21 (Quantum advantage in dimension reduction) . Consider the dimension reduction task with sample dimension N , feature dimension D, repetition number R, and guiding vector quality χ ∈

(0 , 1] defined in Theorem F.19. Using ˜O(RN D 1−χ) samples, a quantum machine with poly(log D) size can solve it with 1/poly(log( D)) error and high success probability, while any classical machine with 

o(D(1 −ζ)χ) size for any constant ζ > 0 cannot solve it with 1/poly(log( D)) error and success probability more than 0.67 . Moreover, the data processing time per sample of the quantum machine is poly(log D).137 The second result shows that if the size of the classical machine is slightly smaller than o(D2χ−1), it would need super-polynomially more samples than a small quantum machine to solve the dynamic dimension reduction task. This separation starts as soon as we have a guiding state of quality χ > 1/2 (i.e., ⃗g ·⃗w ≥ ˜Ω(1 /D 1/4)). In the context of batch processing, this means that quantum machines can solve the task with a few batches of training data, whereas sub-exponential size classical machines require super-polynomially many batches. 

Theorem F.22 (Quantum advantage in dynamic dimension reduction) . Consider the dynamic dimen-sion reduction task with sample dimension N , feature dimension D, repetition number R, guiding vector quality χ ∈ (1 /2, 1] , and sufficient refreshing time τ = ˜O(RN D 1−χ) defined in Theorem F.20. A quan-tum machine with poly(log D) size can use ˜O(RN D 1−χ) samples to solve it with 1/poly(log( D)) error and high success probability, while any classical machine with o(D(1 −ζ)(2 χ−1) ) size for any constant ζ > 0

that solves it with 1/poly(log( D)) error and probability at least 2/3 must collect at least RN ω(1) samples. Moreover, the data processing time per sample of the quantum machine is poly(log D).

Together, Theorems F.21 and F.22 establish unconditional and exponential quantum advantages in the foun-dational task of dimension reduction.  

> c. Quantum algorithm

Here, we prove the quantum algorithm parts of Theorems F.21 and F.22. We construct an algorithm that performs high-dimensional PCA on a small quantum computer. We do so by combining quantum oracle sketching (Theorem D.16), quantum state sketching (Theorem D.24), and a ground state preparation algorithm [84]. We again use the interferometric classical shadow developed in the last section (Theorem F.16) to readout the principal component |w⟩ into a classical representation, which is used for predicting the 1D representations of all possible test vectors.

Lemma F.24 (Quantum ground state preparation [84, Corollary 9]) . Let D be a large integer. Let H ∈ RD×D

be a real, symmetric matrix with the smallest eigenvalue λ1 and corresponding unit eigenvector ⃗w ∈ R D and second smallest eigenvalue λ2 such that λ2 − λ1 ≥ ∆. Let ⃗g ∈ R D be a unit vector such that ⃗g ·⃗w ≥ q. There exists a quantum algorithm that applies a unitary V such that

Recall that in Theorem D.23, we have shown that ˜O(RN s 5) samples suffice to implement the block encoding of an s-sparse matrix. In addition, Theorem D.24 shows that we can use ˜O(RN ) samples to prepare the quantum state of any vector. We combine Theorem F.24 with Theorem D.23, Theorem D.24, and interferometric classical shadow (Theorem F.16) to prove Theorem F.23.

dimension to apply quantum oracle and state sketching. We define H = −XT X ∈ RD×D, which is real and symmetric. The smallest eigenvalue of H corresponds to the largest singular value of X, and therefore the ground state of H is indeed the principal component ⃗w of X. The gap between the smallest and second smallest eigenvalues of H is ∆ by definition. The block encoding of H can be implemented with two queries to the block encoding of X and its inverse. > d. Classical hardness

In this section, we prove the two classical hardness results in Theorems F.21 and F.22. We will construct a (dynamic) dimension reduction task with N = D. Hence, for simplicity, we will use N alone throughout this section. We prove the classical hardness results by constructing a specific dimension reduction task using Theorem E.48. Solving it amounts to solving the (dynamic) Noisy Oracle Property Estimation (NOPE) task defined in Section E (Theorems E.1 and E.26), whose classical hardness we have already proved in Theorem E.29 and Theorem E.28. This gives us two classical hardness results. The first result (Theorem F.26) follows from the distributional sample-space lower bound of NOPE (Theorem E.29) and shows that any classical learning algorithm that wants to achieve success probability 0 .67 in dimension reduction with the same number of samples quantum algorithms need must have Ω( D(1 −ζ)χ) size for any constant ζ > 0. The second result (Theorem F.25) follows from Theorem E.28 and shows that when the dimension reduction task is dynamic, any classical learning algorithm will need a number of samples super-polynomial in N if it has size o(D(1 −ζ)(2 χ−1) ) for any constant 

ζ > 0. The dynamic result follows a similar reasoning as the linear system and binary classification case. We therefore prove it first. The non-dynamic result requires a slight modification, which we will detail at the end of this section. The general idea of proving these results is to embed the quantum circuit that solves dynamic NOPE from Theorem E.45 into a dimension reduction task via Theorem E.48. The 1D representation ξ(⃗x) of a fixed, sparse test vector ⃗x encodes the oracle property B ∈ { 0, 1} that we want to estimate in (dynamic) NOPE. Then we show that given any classical algorithm that solves the dimension reduction task, we can use it to construct a classical algorithm that solves (dynamic) NOPE. In the (dynamic) NOPE task, we take the oracle property function f to be K-Forrelation (Theorem E.38) and the noisy encoding function g to be the inner product (Theorem E.41). In the following, we construct the data generation process DN,K,R  

> DR

(B), B ∈ { 0, 1} for dimension reduction, where N is both the sample dimension and the feature dimension (i.e., D = N ), K = Θ(1) is the constant in the Forrelation that we will embed, R is an integer that specifies the repetition number, and B indicates whether the 1D representation of that fixed test vector has a large or small value (see Theorem E.48). The goal is to solve for B. It helps to compare this construction to the dynamic NOPE data generation process defined in Section E 4. Given N , we define another large integer N ′ as follows. Let T = Θ(log 3 N ′ log log N ′) be the total number of two-qubit gates and diagonal gates given in Theorem E.45 and let n′ = log N ′ + O(log log N ′) be the number of qubits in Theorem E.45. To properly embed that circuit into the N -dimensional training dataset, we define 

N ′ such that N = ( T + 1)2 n′+1 = N ′ · polylog N ′ as in Theorem E.48. This implies N ′ = N/ polylog N . In particular, we define D2(α,β ) in the following way. We first sample a random row of the matrix X as follows. Note that the linear space in which the matrix X lives factorizes as |t⟩ | ψ⟩ where |t⟩ is the clock register and |ψ⟩

is the ( n′ +1)-qubit register that the realified quantum circuit runs on. We sample a clock time t ∼ Uniform([ T ]) and the matrix is reduced to a specific gate in the ( n′ + 1)-qubit subspace (either a fixed two qubit gate or a diagonal gate that depends on Y (α,β )). The remaining subspace further factorizes into |x, α, β, k ⟩ and the rest of the working qubits. We sample a random basis of this ( n′ + 1)-qubit subspace by plug in the specific ( α, β ) that we have already sampled, sample x ∼ Uniform([ KN ′]) , k ∼ Uniform([ b]), and sample a computational basis of the rest of the working qubits uniformly random. This together specifies and thus samples a row i of the matrix 

X. Theorem F.25 (Classical hardness of dynamic dimension reduction) . Let ζ > 0 be any constant. Let 

N, D be the sample and feature dimension of a dimension reduction task and R, τ = ˜O(RN D 1−χ) be its repetition number and refreshing time. Given a guiding vector of constant quality at least χ > 1/2, any randomized classical learning algorithm that solves the task with error ϵ = Θ(1 / log 2(N )) and success probability at least 2/3 must have sample complexity 

M ≥ RN ω(1) (F134)

S ≤ o(D(1 −ζ)(2 χ−1) ). (F135) 142 Theorem F.25 directly implies the classical hardness part of Theorem F.22. Together with the quantum algorithm result Theorem F.13, this completes the proof of the quantum advantage claim in Theorem F.12. However, to obtain the claim in Theorem F.21 where 2 χ − 1 is improved to χ, we need to design a different data generation process based on the distributional sample-space lower bound (Theorem E.29) that we will detail later.

Together with the quantum algorithm result Theorem F.23, this proves the quantum advantage claim in dynamic dimension reduction (Theorem F.22). Finally, we prove the following result that implies the classical hardness part of Theorem F.21. 

Theorem F.26 (Classical hardness of dimension reduction) . Let ζ > 0 be any constant. Let N, D be the sample and feature dimension of a dimension reduction task and R be its repetition number. Given a guiding vector of constant quality χ ∈ (0 , 1] , using ˜O(RN D 1−χ) samples, any randomized classical learning algorithm with space complexity 

S ≤ o(D(1 −ζ)χ) (F143) 

cannot solve the dimension reduction task with error ϵ = Θ(1 / log 2(N )) and success probability more than 

0.67 .

The origin of the difference between the Dχ exponent and the D2χ−1 exponent is as follows. The ground state preparation algorithm has query complexity Q ∼ 1/(⃗g ·⃗w) ∼ D (1 −χ)/2. Due to the quadratic slowdown of quantum oracle sketching (caused by the incoherent random sampling of classical data), we have sample complexity M ∼ N Q 2 ∼ N D 1−χ. The classical sample-space lower bound in Theorem E.29 then provides the classical space lower bound S ≳ DQ C /M ∼ D · N/ (N D 1−χ) ∼ Dχ as claimed. This is the intuition behind Theorem F.26. As for the dynamic version (Theorem F.25), the learning XOR lemma (Theorem E.30) incurs an additional T1 ∼ M/N ∼ D1−χ factor in the space lower bound due to interleaving problem instances. This leads to a final space lower bound of S ≳ Dχ/T 1 ∼ Dχ−(1 −χ) = D2χ−1 as in Theorem F.25.
